slides.title: Next.js 15 & Fastify 5 Masterclass
slides.subtitle: Designing High-Throughput Web Architectures with React Server Components, Custom Servers, and Async Lifecycle Hooks

slides.name: Aravind Govindhasamy
slides.title: Software Developer & Infrastructure Architect
slides.experience: 3 years in full-stack engineering, .NET, Python, and IoT systems
slides.oss_experience: Performance engineering, server-side optimization, and CI/CD pipelines

slides.title: Next.js + Fastify Synergy
slides.subtitle: Combining High-Velocity Frontend Routing with Industrial-Strength API Execution

slides.title: The Performance Bottleneck of Default Servers

box.title: ⚠️ Limits of the Default Next.js Production Runtime
list: Standard Node HTTP Server: The default production runner wraps Next.js in a basic Node HTTP listener with high execution overhead.
JSON Serialization Bottleneck: Standard API handlers rely on dynamic `JSON.stringify` runs, blocking the single thread under load.
Lack of Request Lifecycle Hooks: No native support for centralized pre-routing auth check gates, logging buffers, or parsing pipelines.
Monolithic API Overhead: Forcing performance-critical endpoints through Next.js serverless route handlers introduces cold starts and memory pressure.

slides.title: Default Next.js Server vs. Custom Fastify Server
leftPoints: 📦 Standard Node.js wrapper with zero-config deployment.
🐌 API route requests suffer from dynamic path resolution overhead.
🧱 Lacks prevalidation hooks, requiring ad-hoc security filters.
📝 Dynamic payload serialization blocks the event loop under heavy throughput.
rightPoints: 🚀 Optimized HTTP engine running on pre-compiled schema validation (AJV).
🪝 Robust middleware and lifecycle hook pipeline (onRequest, preValidation).
🔍 Low-overhead asynchronous structured logging out of the box (Pino).
⚡ Handles high-volume REST/Websocket APIs while hosting the SSR page router.

note.text: By wrapping Next.js in Fastify, we decouple standard page rendering from raw API delivery, maximizing resource efficiency.

slides.title: Next.js 15 & Fastify 5 Request Flow Lifecycle
slides.content:
[ Client HTTP / Web Socket Request ]
                       │
                       ▼
      ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
      │     Fastify Ingress Router       │
      └────────────────┬─────────────────┘
                       ├─────────────────────────────────┐
                       ▼ (API Routes /ws)                ▼ (SSR / RSC pages)
      ┌──────────────────────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
      │      Fastify Hook Engine         │     │     Next.js Request Handler      │
      │  1. onRequest (Authentication)   │     │  1. Check static data cache      │
      │  2. preValidation (AJV Schema)   │     │  2. Render Server Components     │
      │  3. Handler execution            │     │  3. Stream HTML / RSC payloads   │
      └────────────────┬─────────────────┘     └────────────────┬─────────────────┘
                       │                                        │
                       ▼ (Fast Serialization)                   ▼ (React Suspense Stream)
      [ Pre-compiled JSON response payload ]   [ Chunked HTML streamed response ]

note.text: Fastify routes intercept API requests directly, while page routes are forwarded seamlessly to the Next.js compilation handler.

slides.title: Next.js 15 Core Foundations
slides.subtitle: Harnessing React 19, Server-First Rendering, and Rust Compilers

slides.title: React Server Components (RSC) Paradigm
slides.imageAlt: React components inside a modern code IDE
slides.caption: RSC: Decoupling component execution from client side hydration
slides.content:
🏗️ Server-First Component Architecture

React Server Components represent a paradigm shift, enabling components to render exclusively on the server before shipping lightweight UI data.

  • 📉 Zero Bundle Size - NPM packages imported inside Server Components stay on the server, saving client bytes.
  • 🔌 Direct DB Access - Query databases, call internal APIs, and read filesystem arrays directly within the component.
  • 🔒 Secure Execution - Keep private tokens and secrets away from client-side JS bundles automatically.

slides.title: Streaming with Suspense & React Server Components

box.title: 🌊 Enhancing Perceived Performance with Dynamic Loading
list: Incremental Hydration: Stream individual parts of the page structure to the browser as soon as they compile.
Layout Isolation: Static page frames load instantly, while slower data-fetching widgets display Loading skeletons.
React Suspense integration: Declarative fallback boundaries wrap asynchronous components to prevent blocking page transitions.
RSC Payload Streaming: The server transmits a virtual DOM representation over a text stream, which React merges into the browser DOM live.

slides.title: Server Components vs. Client Components
leftPoints: Direct server-side data fetching and filesystem access.
Keeps npm dependencies out of the client JS bundle.
Executes once on the server during rendering.
Cannot use hooks (useState, useEffect) or event listeners.
rightPoints: Hydrates in the browser to enable complete interactivity.
Full access to state, lifecycle effects, and web APIs.
Can be nested inside Server Components as leaf nodes.

note.text: Understanding the boundary between server logic and client interactivity is key to Next.js 15 mastery.

box.title: 🦀 Rust-Powered Incremental Compilation
list: Development Startup Speed: Turbopack boots the dev environment up to 7x faster than Webpack.
Fast Refresh Stability: File changes trigger microsecond-level hot updates without resetting client application state.
On-Demand Compilation: Only compiles files requested by the active browser tab, reducing memory overhead.
Webpack Compatibility: Out-of-the-box compatibility with standard Next.js loaders and CSS configurations.

box.title: ⚡ The 4 Cache Layers of Next.js 15
list: Request Memoization: Deduplicates fetch requests targeting identical endpoints within a single render pass.
Data Cache: Stores fetched server data across page reloads. Persisted at the framework level until revalidated.
Full Route Cache: Compiles and caches entire static HTML pages at build time to enable instant delivery.
Router Cache: Client-side memory cache that stores visited pages, enabling instantaneous backward and forward navigation.

slides.title: Custom Fastify 5 Server Setup
slides.subtitle: Writing a custom Node entrypoint to register Next.js into the Fastify request lifecycle

box.title: 🔧 Bootstrapping next() inside Fastify
🪝 Centralizing Request Processing Pipeline
list: onRequest: Runs immediately after parsing headers. Perfect for global authorization validations.
preValidation: Fired before body parsers and AJV validation. Best for decrypting payloads or resolving tokens.
preHandler: Executes after body parsing but before the route controller logic. Used for role-based permissions.
onResponse: Triggers after sending packets to clients. Ideal for latency metrics tracking and connection cleanup.

slides.title: AJV Schema Validation & Serialization
slides.imageAlt: Abstract security lock overlaying digital data lines
slides.caption: Strict schema contracts maximize API stability and speed
slides.content:
🛡️ Pre-compiled JSON Validation

Fastify uses AJV (Another JSON Schema Validator) to precompile routes. This guarantees high-speed validation before processing payload bytes.

  • ⚡ AJV Schema Compilation - Translates JSON schemas into optimized JS validation functions at startup.
  • 🛡️ Input Hardening - Automatically strips unmapped input fields to block malicious parameter injection.
  • 📈 Maximum Performance - Eliminates dynamic runtime checks, ensuring near-instant validation cycles.

box.title: 📝 Non-Blocking JSON Stream Outputs
list: Zero Event-Loop Blockage: Pino logs to stdout asynchronously using internal system memory buffers.
Structured JSON Out: Every trace outputs as a single-line JSON log, ready for ELK or OpenSearch ingestion.
Extreme Low Overhead: Up to 10x faster than legacy logging frameworks like Winston or Morgan.
Contextual Serialization: Automatically sanitizes logs by stripping private header variables and passwords.

slides.title: Mutations & Interactive State
slides.subtitle: Mastering Server Actions, Form Validation, and Optimistic UI Updates

box.title: 🏗️ Server-Side Mutation Functions called from the Client
🛡️ Schema Enforcement at the Mutation Boundary
list: Server-Side Parsing: Validate inputs inside Server Actions using schemas to reject bad payloads.
Shared Models: Share the validation schema file between client-side form libraries and server code.
Detailed Error Reporting: Map validation failures directly to form fields for quick feedback.
Prevent SQL Injection: Enforcing strict schemas limits incoming data types, neutralizing injection paths.

box.title: ⚡ Building Fluid Interfaces during Asynchronous Actions
⚡ Combining Instant HTML Shells with Dynamic Streams
list: Dynamic Holes: Pages build a static layout shell at compilation, leaving placeholders for dynamic segments.
Instant Load: Static shells load immediately, serving layouts, headers, and cached components instantly.
Suspense Streaming: Slower database queries load in the background, streaming into placeholders when ready.
SEO Benefits: Core metadata and page layout load instantly, boosting SEO indexes and crawler scores.

slides.question: Which feature combines static shells with dynamic streams of server components in Next.js 15?
slides.explanation: Partial Prerendering (PPR) compiles a static page shell immediately and streams in dynamic React Server Component inputs when they become ready.

slides.title: Complex UI Routing & Layout Architectures
slides.subtitle: Unlocking Dashboards and Modals with Parallel and Intercepting Routes

box.title: 📊 Rendering Independent Sub-pages Simultaneously
list: Slot Architecture: Use the `@folder` naming convention to define independent slots in layouts.
Independent States: Each slot maintains its own error boundaries, loading loops, and suspenses.
Flexible Matching: Serve unique pages for specific slots based on sub-route URL conditions.
Conditional Routing: Serve distinct slots based on user permissions or feature flags.

note.text: The slots feed into layout parameters: export default function Layout({ dashboard, analytics })

box.title: 🖼️ Modals and Feeds with Shareable URLs
list: Soft Navigation: Intercepts sub-routes when navigated internally, opening content in a modal.
Hard Navigation: Reloading or sharing the URL loads the target page directly, skipping the modal wrapper.
Directory Syntax: Use `(.)` to match current directory paths, `(..)` for parent paths, and `(...)` for root matches.
Context Preservation: Keeps background elements active, avoiding state loss when inspecting content.

slides.title: Dynamic SEO & Metadata API
slides.imageAlt: Charts and analysis metrics shown on a screen
slides.caption: Structured SEO meta headers adapt dynamically
slides.content:
🔍 Generating Dynamic Metadata

Next.js 15's Metadata API allows you to define page head parameters dynamically, reading database records directly.

  • 📜 generateMetadata Hook - Fetch parameters asynchronously within pages to return dynamic SEO fields.
  • 🛡️ Avoid Double Fetching - React automatically aggregates duplicate fetch requests, saving database load.
  • 🌍 Rich Meta Support - Generates OpenGraph cards, Twitter handles, and canonical structures natively.

slides.title: High-Throughput API Design
slides.subtitle: Optimizing Serialization, Rate-Limiting, and Security Headers in Fastify

box.title: ⚡ Building High-Speed Response Pipelines
list: Fast JSON Stringify: Precompiles response schemas into flat serialization scripts.
Avoid Dynamic Parsing: Speeds up JSON serialization, running up to 5x faster than standard JS methods.
Data Leaks Protection: Schema declarations act as output filters, preventing private keys from leaking.
Lower Memory Footprint: Precompiled routes skip runtime object audits, keeping GC cycles light.

box.title: 🧩 Reusing Data Models across Request/Response Validation
🧱 Shielding APIs from DDoS and Cross-Origin Threats
list: fastify-rate-limit: Restricts traffic dynamically using sliding window counters. Integrates with Redis backends.
fastify-cors: Declares strict rules to restrict cross-origin script requests to trusted domains.
Graceful Degradation: Returns structured HTTP 429 errors under heavy load, protecting database resources.
Client Identification: Identifies requests accurately using reverse proxy headers (Cloudflare CF-Connecting-IP).

slides.title: JSON.stringify vs. fast-json-stringify
leftPoints: Checks data types dynamically at runtime.
Serializes all object properties, risking credential leaks.
Blocks the Node event loop on large payloads.
Simple and universal, but slow under heavy load.
rightPoints: Precompiles static schemas into optimized JS functions.
Serialized response speeds run up to 5x faster.
Requires upfront schema maintenance, built for scale.

note.text: By enforcing static response schemas, Fastify delivers maximum performance under peak loads.

slides.title: Middleware & Security Hardening
slides.subtitle: Securing Next.js Edge Execution and Fastify Route Enforcements

box.title: ⚡ Lightweight Edge Routing Guards
list: Edge Runtime Context: Middleware runs on V8 sandboxes, avoiding bulky Node.js runtime layers.
Token Verification: Inspects cookies and JWTs to restrict route access before rendering.
Redirect Orchestration: Redirects unauthenticated users immediately, preventing resource waste.
Headers Modification: Injects security headers and tracking context before passing requests forward.

box.title: 🔑 Route Isolation with preValidation Hooks
🧱 Stopping Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) at the Source
list: Dynamic Nonces: Generate cryptographic keys per request to sign dynamic scripts.
Script Source Restrictions: Limit script sources to authorized CDN endpoints, blocking injection paths.
Helmet integration: Configure `fastify-helmet` to enforce strict CSP rules natively.
Policy Violations Audit: Log and audit CSP violations using automated endpoint collectors.

box.title: 🔒 Scaling Auth Session Checks across Frontend and API Layers
list: Stateless Sessions: Use encrypted JWTs to verify users, eliminating database lookups on every request.
Auth.js Integration: Manage user sessions, providers, and token rotations in Next.js pages.
Secure Cookie Storage: Store JWTs in HTTP-only, secure, same-site cookies to prevent XSS theft.
Shared Decryption Keys: Share validation secrets between Next.js and Fastify, simplifying token checks.

slides.title: Production Observability
slides.subtitle: Monitoring Performance across Routing and API Boundaries

box.title: 📈 Tracking Requests Across Microservice Boundaries
list: Distributed Tracing: Track client actions as they pass through Next.js and Fastify.
Context Propagation: Pass trace contexts via W3C HTTP headers, linking frontend steps to backend logs.
OpenTelemetry Instruments: Capture server response rates, database latencies, and execution bottlenecks.
Visualization: Export tracing telemetry data directly to dashboard systems (Jaeger, Honeycomb).

box.title: ⚙️ Kubernetes Liveness & Readiness Endpoints

slides.question: What is the primary benefit of using Pino over Winston for logging in Fastify?
options: Pino supports formatting colors in local files.
Pino is asynchronously write-optimized, reducing event-loop block time.
Pino does not write to stdout.
Pino is written entirely in Rust.
slides.explanation: Pino utilizes non-blocking stream buffers and structured JSON layouts to minimize overhead and prevent blocking the Node.js event loop.

slides.title: Deployment & Standalone Builds
slides.subtitle: Optimizing Standalone Builds, Containers, and Cluster Scaling

box.title: 📦 Minimizing Production Node Image Weights
list: output: 'standalone': Tells Next.js to package only active backend files into a separate folder.
Dependency Stripping: Ignores devDependencies, dropping final image weights by up to 80%.
Single Entrypoint: Generates `server.js`, ready to run on standard VMs and cloud runtimes.
Simple Integration: Run Next.js's standalone server alongside the Fastify custom server.

box.title: 🐳 Multi-Stage Container Compilations
box.code:
# Stage 1: Build
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Stage 2: Runner
FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
COPY --from=builder /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder /app/.next/static ./.next/static
USER node
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

box.title: 🌍 Dynamic Execution Closer to Users
list: Edge Middleware: Runs auth checks and geo-routing directly at the CDN ingress boundary.
RSC Edge Streaming: Stream server component data directly from regional datacenters, cutting latency.
Web APIs Support: Edge execution uses Web APIs, stripping Node.js dependencies for faster startups.
Global Scaling: Distributes loads across global endpoints, protecting backend database servers.

box.title: 📈 Distributing Client Load across Multi-core CPUs
list: Node Cluster Module: Spawn worker instances matching core counts, multiplying event loop performance.
Port Sharing: Worker processes share the same incoming port, balanced by OS network drivers.
PM2 Runner: Simplify cluster deployment using production monitors like PM2.
Stateless Backends: Store session variables in shared Redis caches, enabling load-balanced requests.

slides.title: QA & Test Framework Integration
slides.subtitle: Writing Server Actions, Mocking Services, and Browser E2E Tests

box.title: ⚡ Testing Server-Side API Functions Directly
🎭 Verifying Complete Client User Journeys
list: Browser Isolation: Runs test suites in headless Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit instances.
Parallel Execution: Runs test pipelines in parallel, cutting total runtimes.
Auto-Waiting: Automatically waits for elements to render, resolving flaky UI tests.
Trace Capture: Captures video recordings and call logs when assertion errors occur.

slides.title: Mocking boundaries with MSW (Mock Service Worker)

box.title: 🚥 Mocking Network Calls at the Service Worker Boundary
list: Network Interception: Intercepts API requests at the browser layer, returning custom payloads.
Declarative Mocks: Define mock API routes once, using them in both tests and local dev servers.
No API Bleeding: Isolates tests from live third-party endpoints, ensuring test predictability.
True HTTP Behavior: Simulates network issues, delays, and HTTP 500 crashes to test error-handling.

slides.question: How does Playwright handle route intercepting during test validation?
options: It compiles testing binaries inside Fastify.
It runs client-side scripts to block browsers.
It mocks browser requests using `page.route()` to return cached JSON.
It requires a physical network gateway.
slides.explanation: Playwright's `page.route()` API intercepts HTTP traffic at the browser process level, allowing tests to mock downstream APIs reliably.

slides.title: Conclusion & Next Steps
slides.subtitle: Combining Frontend Velocity with Backend Performance

box.title: 📈 Key Milestones of the Integrated Stack
list: RSC Evolution: React 19 integration will continue to refine rendering speeds and client bundle sizes.
Fastify 5 Adoption: Leverages updated HTTP processing layers and plugin updates for faster API delivery.
Rust Tooling: Turbopack and SWC will continue to speed up local dev startup and build times.
Edge Orchestration: Deploy Fastify routing layers as regional gateways in front of multi-region servers.

box.title: 📚 Essential Docs and Repositories
list: Next.js Docs: Read nextjs.org/docs for details on App Router routing and caching models.
Fastify Docs: Visit fastify.dev to learn about plugins, hooks, schemas, and ecosystem utilities.
React 19 Portal: Review react.dev to master server components, actions, and interactive hooks.
Sample App Repo: Fork reference implementations on GitHub to deploy containerized versions.

slides.title: Next.js 15 & Fastify Mastery
slides.subtitle: Build fast, scale far.
slides.message:
Thank you for attending!
Check out the docs and start building high-performance full-stack applications today.
